ever, \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) is still relatively small (0.039 eV/Vtet), suggesting that V might still easily migrate out of metastable octahedral sites at this composition.

The value of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct}\rightarrow \mathrm{tet}}\) for \(\mathrm{VO}_2\) was calculated to be the second lowest in Table 3. Unfortunately the valences of V in \(ps-(\mathbf{M})_{\mathrm{tet}}(\mathbf{M}_3)_{\mathrm{oct}}\mathrm{O}_8\) could not be clearly determined using the spin integration method of section 5 because the net spin on the V did not match a spin-polarized configuration consistent with the average formal oxidation state. Instead, the V took on a non-spin-polarized state.

Like V, Cr is calculated to maintain a \(+3.5\) oxidation state in both octahedral and tetrahedral coordination at \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 1 / 2\) (Table 2). At this lithium concentration the projected change in LFSE and SPE is higher for Cr than for V, which is consistent with the greater value of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) calculated for Cr in Table 2. This supports Cr having a stronger preference for octahedral sites at the \(+3.5\) valence than V. At \(+4\) valence Cr was calculated to have the lowest \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) in Table 3. This is consistent with the relatively low projected change in LFSE and SPE for \(\mathrm{Cr^{4 + }}\) moving from octahedral to tetrahedral coordination \((4 / 5)\Delta_0 - (6 / 5)\Delta_{\mathrm{t}})\)

The enhanced stability of \(I\text{-Li}_x(\mathrm{Cr}_{1/2}\mathrm{Mn}_{1/2})\mathrm{O}_2\) reported experimentally can be explained by these results.[53] As \(I\text{-Li}(\mathrm{Cr}_{1/2}\mathrm{Mn}_{1/2})\mathrm{O}_2\) is delithiated, the Mn is oxidized to \(+4\) first according to section 9 and experiment.[55] As already discussed, \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\) is highly stable in octahedral coordination. Cr is oxidized to \(+4\) later in the charge, when Li vacancies that can facilitate TM ion migration are more plentiful. The relatively high value of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct}\rightarrow \mathrm{tet}}\) calculated for \(\mathrm{Cr}^{3.5+}\) in Table 2 probably reflects a strong preference for octahedral coordination at this valence. One would expect Cr to have an even stronger preference for octahedral coordination in \(I\text{-Li}_{1/2}(\mathrm{Cr}_{1/2}\mathrm{Mn}_{1/2})\mathrm{O}_2\) since its valence is \(+3\) which has a \(\mathfrak{t}_{2\mathfrak{g}}^3\) orbital filling—the same orbital filling as \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\).

While \(\mathrm{Cr}^{4+}\) has the lowest \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) in Table 3 and relatively weak ligand-field stabilization, the absolute value of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) (0.54 eV) for \(\mathrm{CrO_2}\) is still higher than that for \(\mathrm{Li}_{1 / 2}\mathrm{CrO}_2\) (0.3 eV). Furthermore, there probably is no longer a driving force to convert to spinel in highly delithiated \(I\text{-Li}_x(\mathrm{Cr}_{1 / 2}\mathrm{Mn}_{1 / 2})\mathrm{O}_2\). The fact that the structural integrity of \(I\text{-Li}_x(\mathrm{Cr}_{1 / 2}\mathrm{Mn}_{1 / 2})\mathrm{O}_2\) is maintained over delithiation attests to a relatively strong octahedral site preference exhibited by Cr at partial lithiation.

It has been observed that when \(\mathrm{Cr}^{6+}\) is formed in delithiated \(I\text{-Li}(\mathrm{Li}_{1/5}\mathrm{Mn}_{2/5}\mathrm{Cr}_{2/5})\mathrm{O}_2\), it spontaneously moves into tetrahedral coordination.[55] This is consistent with the lack of d electrons for \(\mathrm{Cr}^{6+}\) and hence the lack of ligand-field stabilization.

Mn goes from having the lowest \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct}\rightarrow \mathrm{tet}}\) at \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0.5\) (Table 2) to the second highest \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct}\rightarrow \mathrm{tet}}\) at \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0\) (Table 3). The low value of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct}\rightarrow \mathrm{tet}}\) for Mn at \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0.5\) is consistent with a relatively small projected
change in LFSE and SPE equal to the Jahn-Teller splitting \((\alpha)\).<sup>47,48</sup>

As mentioned before, the fact that \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) is lower for Mn than for Ti despite \(\alpha\) being greater than zero runs contrary to expectations based solely on LFSE. According to the simple model of eqs 3-5 in section 8.1, the low energy of \(p s\text{-} (\mathrm{LiMn})_{\mathrm{tet}}(\mathrm{LiMn}_3)_{\mathrm{oct}}\mathrm{O}_8\) compared to \(I\text{-}\mathrm{Li}_{1 / 2}\mathrm{MnO}_2\) can be explained by a lower d barycenter in \(p s\text{-} (\mathrm{LiMn})_{\mathrm{tet}}(\mathrm{LiMn}_3)_{\mathrm{oct}}\mathrm{O}_8\) (eq 6). The d electrons of the tetrahedral \(\mathrm{Mn^{2 + }}\) enter d orbitals that are shifted lower in energy, probably because they are surrounded by less negative charge in a tetrahedral site than in an octahedral one. \(\mathrm{Ti^{4 + }}\) , on the other hand does not have any d electrons, so there is no energy reduction resulting from a lower tetrahedral d barycenter.

Another factor that could make \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) particularly low for Mn in Table 2 is the half-full orbital shells of \(\mathrm{Mn^{2 + }}\) and \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4 + }\) (the products of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + }\) charge disproportionation). In general, half-full or full orbital shells have lower energy than other fillings because the electron-electron repulsion is decreased[64] (this is why in the chemistry of low atomic number elements the electrons arrange so often to produce full "octets"). On this basis one expects \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2 + }\) to have a lower electron-electron repulsion energy due to its half-full d shell[64] (\(\mathrm{e^2t_2^3}\) in tetrahedral coordination). It is also favorable for the levels that result from ligand-field splitting to be half-full or full.[50] Therefore, the half-full \(\mathfrak{t}_{2\mathrm{g}}\) level (\(\mathrm{t}_{2\mathrm{g}}^{3}\)) of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4 + }\) is favorable in this regard as well. The energetic favorability of half-filled levels is another reason \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3 + }\) in many environments is observed to be unstable to both reduction to \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2 + }\) and oxidation to \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4 + }\).[52]

At \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0.5\) there is a large positive change in LFSE \((2\Delta_{\mathrm{o}})\) for the formation of tetrahedral Fe. However, this is counteracted by a large negative change in spin-pairing energy \((-2\Delta_{\mathrm{s}})\). The decrease in spin-pairing energy is caused by one of the low-spin \(\mathrm{Fe}_{\mathrm{oct}}^{2/3+}\) becoming high-spin \(\mathrm{Fe}_{\mathrm{tet}}^{3+}\). Recalling that \(\Delta_{\mathrm{s}}\) can be comparable in magnitude to \(\Delta_{\mathrm{o}}\) in 3d metal oxides,[50,51] the net change in LFSE and SPE given for Fe in Table 2 is probably small. This is consistent with the negative value calculated for \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct} \rightarrow \mathrm{tet}}\) \((-0.17 \mathrm{eV} / \mathrm{Fe} \mathrm{tet})\). However, the net change in LFSE and SPE should still have a positive value since Fe is calculated to be low spin in octahedral coordination \((t_{2g}^{4.5}\) or \(t_{2g}^{4.33})\), indicating that \(|\Delta_{\mathrm{s}|} | < |\Delta_{\mathrm{o}}|\).

\(l\text{-LiFeO}_2\) has been synthesized by ion exchange from \(\mathrm{NaFeO_2}\) but it shows little or no electrochemical activity.65 It was not reported whether this is a result of \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3 + }\) migration; however, the results of Table 2 suggest that Fe should be susceptible to migration out of the TM layer at partial lithiation in \(l\text{-Li}_x\mathrm{FeO}_2\)

At \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0\) Fe was found to be of intermediate stability in octahedral coordination. Unfortunately the valences of the Fe ions could not be clearly determined using the spin integration method of section 5 because the net spin on the Fe did not match a high-spin or low-spin configuration consistent with the average formal oxidation state \((+4)\). This suggests that the d-orbital filling takes on an
Chemical Reviews, 2004, Vol. 104, No. 10
Reed and Ceder
intermediate spin configuration for Fe and/or the assumed oxygen formal oxidation state of \(-2\) is no longer justified due to strong covalency between the Fe and O.

Co has the highest value of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) in Table 2. This is consistent with it having the largest projected change in LFSE \((14 / 5)\Delta_{0} - (6 / 5)\Delta_{\mathrm{t}})\). However, the change in LFSE is somewhat counteracted by a negative change in spin-pairing energy \((-2\Delta_{\mathrm{s}})\). The spin-pairing energy is reduced in the charge disproportionation of Co where four d fillings of \(\mathrm{t}_{2\mathrm{g}}^{5,5}\) change to three \(\mathrm{t}_{2\mathrm{g}}^{5}\) and \(\mathrm{e}^4\mathrm{t}_2^3\). This helps explain why \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) calculated for Co is not as high compared to the other 3d metals as one would expect on the basis of LFSE alone.

At \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0\) Co has the third highest \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\). Its change in LFSE and SPE is \(2\Delta_{\mathrm{o}} - 2\Delta_{\mathrm{s}}\) and the same as Fe at \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0.5\), which has a low \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\). A possible explanation for this is that the magnitude of \(\Delta_0\) is higher and/or the magnitude of \(\Delta_{s}\) is lower for \(\mathrm{Co}^{4 + }\) than for \(\mathrm{Fe}^{3.5 + }\).

The remarkable success of \(\mathrm{LiCoO_2}\) as an electrode material is likely related to the strong intrinsic preference of Co for octahedral sites over the \(+3\) to \(+4\) valences as indicated by the high calculated value of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) in Tables 2 and 3. This strong preference of Co for octahedral sites clearly suggests that Co will not easily migrate through an close-packed oxygen framework at these oxidation states.

Ni is calculated to have the second highest \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) at \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0.5\) and the highest \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) at \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0\). At \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0.5\), Ni is calculated to undergo a charge disproportionation reaction (eq 1) similar to Mn and Co. The projected change in LFSE and SPE is higher for Ni than Cr, which is consistent with the higher value of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) calculated for Ni than Cr in Table 2. On the basis of these calculations, Ni is expected to be the second most stable in octahedral sites at an average valence of +3.5 of the 3d metals from Ti to Cu.

The high value of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) calculated for Ni at \(X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0\) is consistent with the large projected change in LFSE \((12 / 5\Delta_0 - 3 / 5\Delta_{\mathrm{t}})\). However, as with \(\mathrm{Co}^{3.5+}\), the change in LFSE is somewhat counteracted by a negative change in spin-pairing energy \((-2\Delta_{\mathrm{s}})\), which helps explain why \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) calculated for \(\mathrm{Ni}^{4+}\) is not as high compared to the other 3d metals as one would expect on the basis of LFSE alone.

The calculated stability of Ni in octahedral coordination is consistent with the good electrochemical performance observed experimentally for \(\mathrm{Li(MnNi)}\)-\(\mathrm{O}_2\) compounds. The \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\) oxidizes Mn to \(+4\), which as already discussed has a strong preference for octahedral sites. When the average valence of Ni is between \(+3\) and \(+4\), there is a high concentration of Li vacancies that would facilitate ion migration, except for the strong preference of Ni for octahedral sites over this valence range. \(\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\) also most likely has a strong preference for octahedral coordination
due to ligand-field effects, although probably not as strong as \(\mathrm{Ni}^{3+}\) since there is an additional electron in the \(\mathbf{e}_{\mathrm{g}}\) level.

At an average formal valence of \(+3.5\), \(\mathrm{Cu}\) is calculated to have the third lowest \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) equal to \(-0.15\mathrm{eV}\) per tetrahedral \(\mathrm{Cu}\). This suggests that \(\mathrm{Cu}\) at this average valence does not have a strong preference for octahedral sites. At an average formal valence of \(+4\), \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) for \(\mathrm{Cu}\) (1 eV) is ranked fourth highest in Table 3. In practice, such high oxidation states for \(\mathrm{Cu}\) are probably difficult to achieve. However, if it were to occur, a metastable structure like \(l\text{-Li}_x\mathrm{CuO}_2\) should be prone to transformation at partial lithiation according to these results. Unfortunately, the valence of \(\mathrm{Cu}\) in both cases could not be clearly determined using the spin integration method of section 5.
10.11. Overall Trends for 3d Metals

The energy of all the 3d metals entering tetrahedral coordination from the \(l\text{-Li}_x\text{MO}_2\) structure decreases as \(X_{\mathrm{Li}}\) goes from 0 to \(1/2\). This is similar to the defect calculations on Co and Mn in section 4 that found tetrahedral defect energies in the layered structure to decrease for both as Li content increases from 0 to \(1/2\).

At \( X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0.5 \), three of the eight TM ions (Mn, Co, and Ni) were found to undergo a major charge disproportionation reaction (eq 1) when moved from octahedral to tetrahedral coordination. In contrast, at \( X_{\mathrm{Li}} = 0 \) none were found to undergo charge disproportionation.

Table 2 shows a good correlation between the relative energetics of octahedral and tetrahedral site occupancy by a 3d metal ion and the projected change in LFSE for moving a 3d metal ion from octahedral to tetrahedral coordination. From rows 4 (Ti) to 8 (Co) in Table 2 the correlation is perfect; the value calculated for \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) increases along with the projected change in LFSE (from 0 for Ti to \((14 / 5)\Delta_{\mathrm{o}}\) \(-(6 / 5)\Delta_{\mathrm{t}}\) for Co).

The two deviations from this trend are Mn and Fe, which have the two most negative values for \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct}\rightarrow \mathrm{tet}}\). Both of these cases probably have relatively small net changes in LFSE and SPE, which is consistent with a more general association between low values of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct}\rightarrow \mathrm{tet}}\) and low projected changes in LFSE and SPE. The case of Fe illustrates that focusing exclusively on LFSE can be misleading when spin pairing is present, especially in low-spin cations.

Table 3 also shows a good correlation between \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\) and the projected change in LFSE, with the largest changes in LFSE being associated with the highest values of \(\Delta E_{\mathrm{oct - tet}}\).

The results given in Tables 2 and 3 support the decisive role of LFSE in the site preference and most likely the mobility of 3d metal ions in a ccp lattice. For Mn, Ni, and Co the results in Table 2 are consistent with the experimental work of Choi, Manthiram et al. who found increasing resistance of \( l\mathrm{-Li}_{x^{\prime}} \) \( \mathrm{MO}_2 \) against transformation into spinel as M is

11. Conclusions

For phase transformations involving rearrangement of transition-metal cations over octahedral sites within a fixed ccp oxide framework, such as the transformation of layered \(\mathrm{Li}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\) to spinel, the results of this study indicate that the low-energy pathway for transition-metal migration between octahedral sites is through a shared nearest neighbor tetrahedral site (i.e., \(O_h \rightarrow T_d \rightarrow O_h\)) rather than directly between octahedral sites (\(O_h \rightarrow O_h\)). This suggests that the smaller the energy change is for transition-metal ion movement between octahedral and tetrahedral coordination, the more easily the TM ions should be able to rearrange in a ccp oxide when transforming from a metastable structure to a stable one. As a result, the resistance against transformation of metastable transition-metal oxides with a ccp oxygen sublattice (e.g., \(\mathrm{Li}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\) with \(\alpha\)-NaFeO\(_2\) structure) will depend on the relative stability of the transition metal in octahedral coordination compared to tetrahedral coordination. The availability of empty tetrahedra without any cations occupying nearest neighbor face-sharing octahedra is also an important factor in the ability of TM ions to migrate through a ccp oxide. Such tetrahedra provide a relatively low-energy pathway by avoiding the highly repulsive interaction between face-sharing cations.

The energetics of Mn movement between octahedral and tetrahedral sites is found to be particularly sensitive to valence. Of the 3d transition metals from Ti to Cu, Mn is calculated to be the second most stable in octahedral coordination at \(+4\) valence and the least stable at \(+3\) valence. This appears to result from a large difference in ligand-field stabilization between the \(+4\) to \(+3\) states of charge for Mn. The most unfavorable change in LFSE associated with Mn movement from octahedral to tetrahedral coordination occurs when Mn is \(+4\) with three spin-polarized d electrons occupying a half-filled \(\mathrm{t}_{2\mathrm{g}}\) shell in octahedral coordination. In contrast, \(\mathrm{Mn_{oct}^{3 + }(t_{2g}^{3}e_{g}^{1})}\) is calculated to undergo a charge disproportionation reaction (eq 1), forming \(\mathrm{Mn_{tet}^{2 + }(e^2t_2^3)}\) and \(\mathrm{Mn_{oct}^{4 + }}\) \((\mathrm{t}_{2\mathrm{g}}^{3})\), which allows \(\mathrm{Mn^{2 + }}\) movement into tetrahedral coordination with a relatively low change in LFSE.

The low-energy passage of Mn through tetrahedral sites enabled by \(\mathrm{Mn_{oct}}^{3+}\) charge disproportionation appears to underlie the instability of metastable ccp Mn oxides such as the layered \(\alpha\text{-NaFeO}_2\) and orthorhombic structures with electrochemical cycling. In both of these structures \(\mathrm{Mn_{oct}}^{3+}\), which can charge disproportionate, and Li vacancies, which facilitate Mn migration by providing empty tetrahedral sites without face-sharing cations, coexist over the Li concentration range where these structures are not thermodynamically stable.
Spinel-like \(\mathrm{Li}_x\mathrm{Mn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\) on the other hand, maintains its structural integrity, even when it is not thermodynamically stable at high and low lithium content.

This is probably because significant amounts of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\) and Li vacancies do not coexist over ranges of Li concentration where the spinel-like structure is metastable. At low lithium concentrations the Mn are primarily in the \(+4\) oxidation state and consequently have relatively low mobility. At high Li content (\(x \approx 2\) in \(\mathrm{Li}_x\mathrm{Mn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\)) there are insufficient Li vacancies to allow easy Mn rearrangement. When substantial concentrations of \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\) and Li vacancies coexist in \(s\text{-LiMn}_2\mathrm{O}_4\), the spinel structure is thermodynamically stable and hence not adversely effected by the increased mobility of Mn. However, there still is the problem of Mn dissolution into the electrolyte through \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\) charge disproportionation.

LFSE also appears to be a decisive factor in the site preference of other 3d TM ions in a ccp oxide. As with Mn, this should impact the resistance of other metastable 3d TM oxides against transformation. The resistance against transformation imparted by ligand-field effects appears to be epitomized in the case of layered \(\mathrm{Li}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\). Contrary to the case with Mn, the change in LFSE associated with Co movement from octahedral to tetrahedral coordination is highly unfavorable in \(\mathrm{Li}_{1/2}\mathrm{CoO}_2\). This provides impressive stability for layered \(\mathrm{Li}_x\mathrm{CoO}_2\) with electrochemical cycling over Li concentrations where the spinel structure is thermodynamically stable (e.g., \(\mathrm{Li}_{1/2}\mathrm{CoO}_2\)).

The results of this investigation lead to the prediction that, in general, metastable Mn oxide structures with a ccp oxygen sublattice will rapidly transform to stable ccp structures if \(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\) and/or \(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\) coexist with sufficient vacant interstitial sites. This is due to the lack of ligand-field stabilization for \(\mathrm{Mn}_{\mathrm{oct}}^{2+}\) and the susceptibility of \(\mathrm{Mn}_{\mathrm{oct}}^{3+}\) to charge disproportionation, which results in low ligand-field stabilization. This in turn makes tetrahedral sites relatively accessible to Mn, which facilitates cationic rearrangement.

The energy difference between oxide structures with and without Mn in tetrahedral coordination can be fitted reasonably well to a simple model in which the energy difference is broken down into two contributions. One contribution is independent of Mn d-orbital filling and hence Mn valence. It accounts for the change in interaction energy between a \(\mathrm{Mn}^{7+}\) (i.e., \(\mathrm{d}^0\)) ionic core and the surrounding cations as a Mn moves from an octahedral to a tetrahedral site. The other energy contribution is from the changing energy of the filled d orbitals as Mn is moved from octahedral to tetrahedral coordination. The latter energy contribution varies in a piecewise linear fashion with Mn valence and has a peak at \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\), reflecting the ligand-field splitting of octahedrally and tetrahedrally coordinated d orbitals.

The energy contribution from the changing Mn d orbitals as Mn moves from octahedral to tetrahedral coordination can in turn be separated into the change of the Mn d-orbital energy barycenter and the change in ligand-field stabilization energy. While the change in LFSE is determined by the Mn valence, the d-orbital barycenter and the energy of interactions involving the \(\mathrm{Mn}^{7+}\) ionic cores are found to be much more sensitive to cationic ordering.

Reed and Ceder

Using ionic valences found through a large series of calculations on substituted Mn oxides, a qualitative ionization scale between Mn and other 3d metals has been constructed. This scale allows one to predict the valences for Mn (in octahedral and/or tetrahedral coordination) coexisting with another 3d TM cation (in octahedral coordination) in a CCP oxide. This could be useful for designing TM oxide materials with improved kinetic stability over the range of Li concentrations covered by electrochemical cycling.

The findings of this study point to a number of different strategies for producing lithium manganese oxide structures, other than spinel, that resist transformation with electrochemical cycling. If a metastable ccp oxide structure such as \(I\text{-Li}_x\text{MnO}_2\) or \(o\text{-Li}_x\text{MnO}_2\) \((\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{Li}} < 1)\) is used, high concentrations of \(\mathrm{Mn_{oct}}^{3+}\) and Li vacancies should be prevented from coexisting over regions of metastability. This can be accomplished by chemically substituting for Mn with low fixed valence and/or electronegative multivalent cations that can oxidize the \(\mathrm{Mn~e_g}\) orbital. There are many different examples of this approach that have had some success experimentally.[7,18,53-55] Another way of oxidizing the \(\mathrm{Mn~e_g}\) orbital is to introduce vacancies into the Mn sublattice.[66]

Given that \(\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\) is the most stable valence in octahedral coordination, it might be desirable for Mn to be electrochemically cycled between elevated oxidation states centered closer to \(+4\) rather than over the \(+3/+4\) redox range characteristic of \(\mathrm{Li}_x\mathrm{MnO}_2\) structures. Unfortunately at this time there probably are not any electrolytes that can withstand the oxidative strength of Mn at valences higher than \(+4\). An additional problem with Mn charged above \(+4\) in ccp oxides is the possibility of decomposition reactions producing \(\mathrm{O}_2\).

Another strategy is to use a structure with an oxygen sublattice that is different from that of spinel (i.e., non-ccp). For such a structure to transform into spinel the oxygen needs to rearrange, which should make the transformation much more difficult.

One way this has been accomplished experimentally is by using a close-packed oxide structure that is not cubic-close packed.[69,70] Such structures may be more resistant against transformation to spinel, but there still is a network of octahedral and tetrahedral sites that could possibly allow undesirable Mn movement during electrochemical cycling. The phosphoolivine structure is an example of a non-ccp oxide that has shown good reversible capacity with iron (LiFe\(\mathrm{PO}_4\)) however not with Mn (although Mn combined with Fe is reported to perform well).[71] Oxides containing polyanions such as \(\mathrm{PO}_4^{-3}\) offer a rich diversity of structures that might be suitable for use in a positive electrode.[72]

Another option is to use a non-close-packed structure. A more open structure can constrain Mn by eliminating energetically favorable sites it can migrate through (e.g., tetrahedral sites). A layered structure can be made less close packed by pillaring open the Li/vacancy layer with a large cation like \(\mathrm{K}^+\) or with a cluster of atoms.[73] The tunneled Mn oxides are another example of non-close-packed structures. Good cyclability and resistance against transforma
tion to spinel has been achieved by Doeff et al. using such a structure.\(^{74,75}\)
